content:
{
  "title": "FactMask: Dynamic Factuality-Driven Prompt Masking for Large Language Models",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) frequently generate plausible-sounding but factually incorrect (hallucinated) content, especially when prompted ambiguously or with insufficient grounding.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLM factuality is critical for adoption in high-stakes domains. Current prompting methods such as chain-of-thought or retrieval-augmented prompting mitigate hallucination but leave substantial room for error, especially in open-domain scenarios. A paradigm shift in prompt design could yield surprising gains.",
  "method": "We propose FactMask, a prompting framework that dynamically masks or highlights portions of the prompt based on their factual verifiability, as determined by an external knowledge source or a lightweight fact-verification model. Prior to LLM inference, the input prompt is segmented and each segment is annotated with a 'factuality score.' Segments below a threshold are masked (replaced with a [FACT-MASK] token) or explicitly flagged, biasing the model toward conservative generation or explicit fact-checking. This dynamic masking is performed in real time as part of prompt construction, and the LLM is conditioned to recognize and handle [FACT-MASK] tokens through targeted instruction tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate FactMask on open-domain QA datasets (e.g., TriviaQA, NaturalQuestions) and fact-sensitive summarization benchmarks (e.g., XSum, FactCC). Baselines include vanilla, chain-of-thought, and retrieval-augmented prompting. Metrics: factuality (FactScore, human annotation), hallucination rate, and answer accuracy. Ablations will isolate the effect of masking versus annotation."
}
